feedback.text: Hello, I would first like to thank you for your work. The protection given by the European Union is always appreciated. My current problems, however, tend to come from the biggest players, from state to business. A simple example: Identification on the Internet. Many countries are moving towards an identification model, such as the UK or even France. These measures are generally unacceptable and give too much data to the countries in question, all for more than questionable protection (again the UK, which requests third party suppliers to manage identification). Apart from these cases, which hide behind the ‘protection of children’ to track adults, there are two others, at my guest, causing problems on the internet: Disinformation and monopolies. When I write to you, Visa and Mastercard used their quasi-monopoly to bring the echo to Steam and Itch.io, two video game distribution platforms, in order to follow rules of censorship that go beyond the law. It seems to me unthinkable that, nowadays, private companies have such a grip on the digital world that they afford to have more impact than a government. I am aware that the EU is creating its own online payment processor, but I would like to ask you to remain vigilant about these cases. And disinformation. In my view, today, the worst enemy of information on the internet is censorship of opinion. On a huge amount of social media, some topics are only allowed for discussion in one direction. A key example for me is Reddit, in particular the French subreddit by default, r/France (2.5 million users). On this platform, the moderation rules are arbitrary and censorship everything on the right or centre, including entire daily publications. As a result, it is very easy for a lambda internet user to join this kind of forum and never be exposed to the opposite view of moderation, which becomes de facto a police of opinion. That policy does not follow any rule and cannot be challenged anywhere. Another example is YouTube, now the most popular video platform in the world, with rules of monetisation and abstract moderation. A great French youtubeur, Frédéric Molas, has already repeatedly paid the cost of an abstract policy applied without any rigour. Creators lose their source of income and more importantly information is limited. The Twitch platform, on the other hand, is well known to ban content creators of advantage for their opinions only for monitoring their rules, which are also applied only to the platform’s good wishes with little protest. In my view, it is important that the rules on platform moderation are defined, and that they must justify whether or not they follow the rules they themselves impose. If a platform is derogated from its own rules, it should be punished.
